steps.title: Identify what the claim requires as evidence
steps.content: The student's claim distinguishes the film from a “precise retelling” by calling it an “imagined but informed representation.” Evidence must show both that Powers created an imaginative account AND that he grounded it in research about the actual people and events.

steps.title: Find the evidence that supports this distinction
steps.content: D supplies both required elements: Powers had only superficial historical details (proving the account is not a precise retelling), conducted extensive research on the individuals and era (proving it is informed), and deliberately portrayed “what might have happened” (emphasizing the imaginative element). This dual nature—imagination guided by research—directly supports the student's claim. The others address reception, planning, or thematic content but don't establish that the film is both imaginative and informed. That matches choice D — D is correct.
